(**BC**) is a [public university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), New York. It is part of the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") (CUNY) system and enrolled nearly 14,000 students on a 35-acre (14 ha) campus in the [Midwood](/wiki/Midwood "Midwood") and [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush "Flatbush") sections of Brooklyn as of fall 2023.

**Brooklyn College** (**BC**) is a [public university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), New York. It is part of the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") (CUNY) system and enrolled nearly 14,000 students on a 35-acre (14 ha) campus in the [Midwood](/wiki/Midwood "Midwood") and [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush "Flatbush") sections of Brooklyn as of fall 2023.

New York City's first public coeducational [liberal arts college](/wiki/Liberal_arts_college "Liberal arts college"), the college was formed in 1930 by the merger of the Brooklyn branches of [Hunter College](/wiki/Hunter_College "Hunter College") (centered in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")), then a [women's college](/wiki/Women%27s_college "Women's college"), and of the [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York") (also Manhattan), then a [men's college](/wiki/Men%27s_college "Men's college"). Once tuition-free, the city's 1975 fiscal crisis ended the free tuition policy. The college also consolidated to its main campus.

Prominent alumni of Brooklyn College include US senators, federal judges, US financial chairmen, Olympians, CEOs, and recipients of [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award"), [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award"), [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), and [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize").

## College history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: College history")]

### Early decades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early decades")]

Brooklyn College was founded in 1930.[[5]](#cite_note-google1-5) That year, as directed by the [New York City Board of Higher Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Board_of_Higher_Education "New York City Board of Higher Education") on April 22, the college authorized the combination of the [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") branches of [Hunter College](/wiki/Hunter_College "Hunter College"), at that time a city [women's college](/wiki/Women%27s_college "Women's college"), and the [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York"), then a [men's college](/wiki/Men%27s_college "Men's college") (both these branches had been established in 1926).[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-google1930-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The third oldest [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") institution in what would become the City University system, Brooklyn College became the first public coeducational [liberal arts college](/wiki/Liberal_arts_college "Liberal arts college") in New York City.[[9]](#cite_note-google2-9) The school opened in September 1930,[[10]](#cite_note-10) holding separate classes for men and women until their junior years.[[7]](#cite_note-google1930-7)[[11]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-11) Admission would require passing a stringent entrance exam.[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/2016_Brooklyn_College_Library.jpg/250px-2016_Brooklyn_College_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_Brooklyn_College_Library.jpg)

Brooklyn College Library, situated on the East Quad, was designed by campus architect Randolph Evans.

In 1932, [architect](/wiki/Architect "Architect") Randolph Evans drafted a plan for the campus on a substantial plot that his employer owned in Brooklyn's [Midwood](/wiki/Midwood "Midwood") section.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Evans sketched a [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_Architecture "Georgian Architecture") campus facing a central [quadrangle](/wiki/Quadrangle_(architecture) "Quadrangle (architecture)"), and anchored by a library building with a tower.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Evans presented the sketches to the college's then president, Dr. [William A. Boylan](/wiki/William_Boylan "William Boylan"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) who approved the layout.

The land was bought for $1.6 million (equivalent to $37.8 million in 2025),[[16]](#cite_note-inflation-US-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) and construction allotment was $5 million (equivalent to $118 million in 2025).[[16]](#cite_note-inflation-US-16)[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-12) Construction began in 1935.[[18]](#cite_note-18) At the groundbreaking ceremony was [Mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") [Fiorello La Guardia](/wiki/Fiorello_La_Guardia "Fiorello La Guardia") and Brooklyn [Borough President](/wiki/Borough_President "Borough President") Raymond Ingersoll.[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-12) In 1936, United States President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") visited and laid the gymnasium's [cornerstone](/wiki/Cornerstone "Cornerstone").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-12) The new campus opened for the fall 1937 semester.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the 1940s, Boylan, Ingersoll, Roosevelt and La Guardia each became namesake of a campus building.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

During the tenure of its second president, [Harry Gideonse](/wiki/Harry_Gideonse "Harry Gideonse"), from 1939 to 1966, Brooklyn College ranked high nationally in number of alumni with doctorate degrees.[[25]](#cite_note-auto10-25)[[26]](#cite_note-newschool.edu-26) As academics fled [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), nearly a third of refugee historians who were female would at some point work at Brooklyn College.[[9]](#cite_note-google2-9) In 1944, sociologist [Marion Vera Cuthbert](/wiki/Marion_Vera_Cuthbert "Marion Vera Cuthbert") became the first permanent black faculty member appointed at any of the New York municipal colleges.[[27]](#cite_note-27) And in 1956, with [John Hope Franklin](/wiki/John_Hope_Franklin "John Hope Franklin") joining, Brooklyn College became the first "white" college to hire on a permanent basis a historian who was black.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In 1959, still tuition-free, about 8,000 undergraduates were enrolled.[[29]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-29) In 1962, the college joined six other colleges to form the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York"), creating the world's second-largest university.[[30]](#cite_note-autogenerated4-30) In 1983, Brooklyn College named its library the Harry D. Gideonse Library.[[25]](#cite_note-auto10-25)[[31]](#cite_note-auto20-31)

Nevertheless, Gideonse remains a controversial figure in the college's history; as one account noted, he is "either lauded as a hero and great educator in hagiographic accounts . . . or decried by faculty and alumni as an autocrat who stifled academic freedom and students' rights."[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In addition to his curricular and student life reforms, Gideonse was known for his decades-long campaign to ferret out Communists among the college community and his testimony before congressional and state investigating committees during the Second [Red Scare](/wiki/Red_Scare "Red Scare").[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

On the other hand, perhaps retaining the memory of the time when, as a [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") professor, he was unjustly accused of being a Communist and advocating "[free love](/wiki/Free_love "Free love"),"[[39]](#cite_note-39) Gideonse also attacked those who, without evidence, charged faculty, staff and students with being subversives and defended faculty free speech rights against outside critics.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The college's third president, [Francis Kilcoyne](/wiki/Francis_Kilcoyne "Francis Kilcoyne"), served from 1966 to 1967.[[44]](#cite_note-google.com-44) The fourth president, [Harold Syrett](/wiki/Harold_Syrett "Harold Syrett"), resigned due to ill health in February 1969, when George A. Peck was named acting president.[[45]](#cite_note-autosy-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) [John Kneller](/wiki/John_Kneller "John Kneller"), Brooklyn College's fifth president, served from 1969 until 1979.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-218.160-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) These presidents served during what were perhaps the most tumultuous years for Brooklyn College.

During the Vietnam War, as they did on other U.S. campuses, student protests rocked Brooklyn College. President Gideonse, in a 1965 television interview, blamed demonstrations on Communists who were "duping the innocents" into demanding more freedom on campus,[[50]](#cite_note-50) leading the New York Civil Liberties Union to criticize Gideonse for "his efforts to smear student groups at the college with the Communist label."[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Also in 1965, student protests forced the Gideonse administration to rescind new, stricter dress rules that forbade male students from wearing dungarees or sweatshirts on campus at any time and mandated that female students wear skirts and blouses even in extremely cold weather.[[52]](#cite_note-52) After Gideonse's retirement in June 1966,[[53]](#cite_note-53) a newly appointed dean of administration, Dante Negro, said he was not bothered by the students' more casual dress "that makes it hard to distinguish between the sexes," calling it "a passing fad."[[54]](#cite_note-54)

On October 21, 1967, a front-page story in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that the college was virtually closed down by a strike of thousands of students angered by police action against antiwar demonstrators protesting U.S. Navy recruiters earlier in the week.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Five days later, another front-page *Times* story reported that students had agreed to return to classes after an agreement was reached with college administrators after negotiations.[[56]](#cite_note-56) A few days after that, President Kilcoyne walked out when New York Mayor [John V. Lindsay](/wiki/John_V._Lindsay "John V. Lindsay") appeared at the college, citing Lindsay's insult in calling the school "Berkeley East."[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Around the same time, the college's students were involved in campus protests involving racial issues. In May 1968, Brooklyn College news again made the front page of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* when police broke up a 16-hour sit-in at the registrar's office to demand that more Black and Puerto Rican students be admitted to the school.[[59]](#cite_note-59) At trial, a Black Brooklyn judge reacted angrily when one student said they had been reacting to racism and sentenced him and 32 other white students to five days in jail for the sit-in.[[60]](#cite_note-60) In May 1969, 19 or 20 Brooklyn College students faced criminal charges in connection with campus disorders during the spring semester, including raids in which students allegedly ransacked files and smoke-bombed the library.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In late April 1970, students demanding more open admissions and racial diversity staged a sit-in at President Kneller's office, holding him and five deans there for several hours.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The next week, in early May 1970, students seized the president's office and other buildings during a [student strike](/wiki/Student_strike "Student strike") upon the [Kent State shootings](/wiki/Kent_State_shootings "Kent State shootings") and the [Cambodian Campaign](/wiki/Cambodian_Campaign "Cambodian Campaign").[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) President Kneller terminated classes, but kept campus buildings open for students and faculty, obtaining a court order against students occupying buildings.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In October 1974, 200 Hispanic students took over the registrar's office to protest President Kneller's appointment of a chair of the Puerto Rican Studies Department different from that of the person selected by a faculty search committee.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Defying a judge's temporary court order to leave the building, the protesters were supported at a rallies outside Boylan Hall by many student groups and the alumni association, but Kneller refused to rescind his controversial appointment.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) Protests flared up again in the spring 1975 semester with another takeover of the registrar's office.[[74]](#cite_note-74) By January 1976, the college's faculty union voted "no confidence" in Kneller, charging that he "consistently ignored faculty rights" and failed to provide leadership.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Brooklyn College, along with the rest of CUNY, shut down for two weeks in May and June 1976 as the university was unable to pay its bills.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Amid New York City's financial crisis, near bankruptcy, Brooklyn College's campus in [downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") closed, leaving the Midwood campus as the Brooklyn College's only campus.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In the fall of 1976, with some 30,000 undergraduates enrolled, the college charged tuition for the first time.[[29]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-29)

In January 1978, the college's Faculty Council approved a vote of "no confidence" in President Kneller on Wednesday and recommended to the Board of Higher Education that he be replaced.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Brooklyn College's sixth president was [Robert Hess](/wiki/Robert_Hess_(college_president) "Robert Hess (college president)"), who served from 1979 to 1992.[[79]](#cite_note-cambridge.org-79)[[80]](#cite_note-auto1-80)[[81]](#cite_note-auto-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Hess initiated major changes in the college curriculum, mandating a standard core for all students.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) Implementation of the new curriculum was aided by a large grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities.[[85]](#cite_note-85) By 1984, in a national report's otherwise gloomy assessment of humanities education, Brooklyn College was singled out as "a bright spot" among American universities for stressing the study of the humanities.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In a 1988 survey of thousands of American college deans, Brooklyn College ranked 5th in providing students with a strong general education, and was the only public institution among the top five.[[80]](#cite_note-auto1-80)[[81]](#cite_note-auto-81) As of 1989, Brooklyn College ranked 11th in the US, and ahead of six of the eight [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") universities, by number of graduates who had acquired doctoral degrees.[[87]](#cite_note-87) At Brooklyn College being called "the poor man’s Harvard," President Hess quipped, "I like to think of Harvard as the rich man’s Brooklyn College."[[88]](#cite_note-88)

Even as Brooklyn College rebounded academically, it suffered a severe budget crunch in the 1988–1989 school year due to reductions in state financing; this resulted in fewer courses, larger classes, no new faculty and staff hirings, supply shortages, and deferral of maintenance of buildings and grounds.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

[Vernon Lattin](/wiki/Vernon_Lattin "Vernon Lattin") was the seventh president of Brooklyn College, from 1992 to 2000.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)
During Lattin's tenure, Brooklyn College began a complete overhaul of campus buildings[[95]](#cite_note-95) and vastly improved computer and Internet access for students and faculty.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97) The college returned to intercollegiate sports competition[[98]](#cite_note-98) and the college chess team won U.S. and international championships.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100) Lattin was also president at the time of the first individual million-dollar donation to the Brooklyn College Foundation.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/2016_Brooklyn_College_campus.jpg/960px-2016_Brooklyn_College_campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_Brooklyn_College_campus.jpg)

View from the West Quad looking onto the East Quad (from left to right, James Hall, Boylan Hall, the library, Ingersoll Hall, and Roosevelt Hall)

Brooklyn College's leafy East Quad looks much like it did when it was originally constructed.[[5]](#cite_note-google1-5)

Whitman Hall, the former performing arts facility which included as its largest venue the George Gershwin Theatre, was demolished and replaced by The Leonard & Claire Tow Center for the Performing Arts, for which ground was broken in 2011.[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-12) The performing arts center was named for alumni [Leonard](/wiki/Leonard_Tow "Leonard Tow") and Claire Tow, who gave $10 million to the college.[[5]](#cite_note-google1-5)[[102]](#cite_note-102) Other changes to the original design include the demolition of Plaza Building, due to its inefficient use of space, poor ventilation, and significant maintenance costs.[[103]](#cite_note-103) To replace the Plaza Building, the college constructed West Quad Center, designed by the notable [Uruguayan](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") architect [Rafael Viñoly](/wiki/Rafael_Vi%C3%B1oly "Rafael Viñoly"). The new building contains classroom space, offices, gymnasiums and a swimming pool. It houses the offices of Registration, Admissions, Financial Aid, and the Department of [Physical Education](/wiki/Physical_Education "Physical Education") and Exercise Science.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The grounds contain a [quadrangle](/wiki/Quadrangle_(architecture) "Quadrangle (architecture)") with grassy areas and trees. New [façades](/wiki/Fa%C3%A7ade "Façade") are being constructed on Roosevelt and James halls where they once connected with Plaza Building. The 2009–10 [CUNYAC](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York_Athletic_Conference "City University of New York Athletic Conference") championship men's basketball team now plays its home games in the West Quad Center.

This followed a major $70 million library renovation completed in 2003 that saw the library moved to a temporary home while construction took place.[[5]](#cite_note-google1-5) The Brooklyn College library is now located in its original location in a completely renovated and expanded LaGuardia Hall.

From 2000 to 2009 when he retired, [Christoph M. Kimmich](/wiki/Christoph_M._Kimmich "Christoph M. Kimmich") was the eighth president of Brooklyn College.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) In the 2003 edition of *The Best 345 Colleges*, the *[Princeton Review](/wiki/Princeton_Review "Princeton Review")* named Brooklyn College as the most beautiful campus in the country and ranked it fifth in the nation for "Best Academic Bang for the Buck".[[30]](#cite_note-autogenerated4-30)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

[Karen L. Gould](/wiki/Karen_L._Gould "Karen L. Gould") was named the ninth president of Brooklyn College in 2009.[[108]](#cite_note-autogenerateda-108) Among her accomplishments were creating a new graduate film school and four new academic schools, new athletic fields, and increasing enrollment in the sciences.[[109]](#cite_note-109) Her tenure was marked by repeated free speech controversies involving Israel that drew both criticism and praise.[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116) After 42 years in higher education, Gould announced her retirement in 2015.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

[Michelle Anderson](/wiki/Michelle_Anderson "Michelle Anderson") became the 10th president of Brooklyn College in 2016.[[118]](#cite_note-118) In 2016, Brooklyn College announced a new home for the Koppelman School of Business, with the planned construction of a new building, Koppelman Hall, on property adjacent to the 26-acre campus bought in 2011. This increased the campus size to 35 acres.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

For four straight years, starting in 2018, [U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report") named Brooklyn College the most ethnically diverse college in the North Region.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Shortly after [October 7th](/wiki/October_7_attacks "October 7 attacks"), [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") member [Inna Vernikov](/wiki/Inna_Vernikov "Inna Vernikov") of the [48th City Council District](/wiki/New_York_City%27s_48th_City_Council_district "New York City's 48th City Council district") was arrested for bringing a gun to a [counterprotest](/wiki/Counter-protest "Counter-protest") at a pro-Palestine rally.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

On May 7, 2025, there was a [Pro-Palestine protest](/wiki/Pro-Palestine_protests_in_the_United_States "Pro-Palestine protests in the United States") on the East Quad Campus.[[123]](#cite_note-123) It ended with police force with a crackdown on the demonstration.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Brooklyn_College_East_Quad_Renovation.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_College_East_Quad_Renovation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_College_East_Quad_Renovation.jpg)

The East Quad under renovation.

Starting on December 22, 2025, the East Quad had recently undergone a renovation.[[125]](#cite_note-125) However, students complained and criticized the action as inconvenient as it hinders their ability to go to their classes on time.[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127)

## Public transit access

### Undergraduate curriculum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Undergraduate curriculum")]

Beginning in 1981, the college instituted a group of classes that all undergraduates were required to take, called "Core Studies".[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-12) The classes were: Classical Origins of Western Culture, Introduction to Art, Introduction to Music, People, Power, and Politics, The Shaping of the Modern World, Introduction to Mathematical Reasoning and Computer Programming, Landmarks of Literature, Chemistry, Physics, Biology, Geology, Studies in African, Asian, and Latin American Cultures, and Knowledge, Existence and Values.[[130]](#cite_note-132)

In 2006, the Core Curriculum was revamped, and the 13 required courses were replaced with 15 courses in 3 disciplines, from which students were required to take 11.[[131]](#cite_note-133) In the fall of 2013, Brooklyn College embarked on CUNY's new general education alternative, the Pathways curriculum, consisting of three components: Required Core (four courses), Flexible Core (six courses) and College Option (four courses)—totaling 42 credits.[[132]](#cite_note-134) Brooklyn College offers over a hundred majors varying from the visual arts to Women's Studies.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

### Division of Graduate Studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Division of Graduate Studies")]

The Division of Graduate Studies at Brooklyn College was established in 1935 and offers more than seventy programs in the arts, education, humanities, sciences, and computer and social sciences. Among those programs is the Graduate theatre program, which is the top ranked in the CUNY system and 14th in the United States; faculty include [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award") nominee Justin Townsend.[[134]](#cite_note-136)[[135]](#cite_note-137)

### B.A.–M.D. program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: B.A.–M.D. program")]

The Brooklyn College [B.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts")–[M.D.](/wiki/Doctor_of_Medicine "Doctor of Medicine") program is an eight-year program affiliated with [SUNY Downstate Medical Center](/wiki/SUNY_Downstate_Medical_Center "SUNY Downstate Medical Center"). The program follows a rigorous selection process, with a maximum of 15 students selected every year. Each student selected to the program receives a Brooklyn College Presidential Scholarship. B.A.–M.D. students must engage in [community service](/wiki/Community_service "Community service") for three years, beginning in their lower sophomore semester. During one summer of their undergraduate studies, students are required to volunteer in a clinical setting where they are involved in direct [patient care](/wiki/Patient_care "Patient care"). B.A.–M.D. students are encouraged to major in the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") or [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences").[[136]](#cite_note-bamd-138)

### The Scholars Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Scholars Program")]

The Scholars Program is home to a small number of students with strong writing ability and academic record. Being the oldest honors program in the [CUNY](/wiki/CUNY "CUNY") system, The Scholars Program has served as a model for many other honors programs nationwide. It was established in 1960 and is an interdisciplinary liberal arts program. The program offers honors-level Core courses and seminars as well as small, personalized classes. Upon graduation from Brooklyn College, many Scholars continue their education in competitive programs at top-ranked universities like Princeton, Yale, and New York University. The program accepts incoming freshmen in addition to matriculated sophomores and transfer students (up to 48 credits). Once admitted, they receive a Brooklyn College Foundation Presidential Scholarship of up to $4,000 for every year of their undergraduate study at Brooklyn College and a laptop computer.[[137]](#cite_note-139)

### Coordinated Engineering Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Coordinated Engineering Program")]

The Coordinated Honors Engineering Program offers a course of study equivalent to the first two years at any engineering school. Students who maintain the required academic level are guaranteed transfer to one of the three coordinating schools—[New York University Tandon School of Engineering](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering"), City College of New York School of Engineering, and the College of Staten Island Engineering Science Program—to complete their bachelor's degree in engineering. Coordinating Engineering students have also transferred to the [New York Institute of Technology](/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Technology "New York Institute of Technology"), [Stony Brook University](/wiki/Stony_Brook_University "Stony Brook University"), [University of Wisconsin](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin "University of Wisconsin"), [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan"), [Cooper Union](/wiki/Cooper_Union "Cooper Union"), and the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"). Students admitted as incoming First-Year receive a Brooklyn College Foundation Presidential Scholarship that provides full tuition for their two years of full-time undergraduate study in the Coordinated Engineering Program. As members of the Honors Academy, Engineering Honors students take advantage of individual advising, faculty consultation, and early registration. In the Commons they find study facilities, computer access, academic, scholarship, internship, and career opportunities, and, above all, intellectual stimulation among other talented students like themselves. Students applying to the Engineering Honors Program will also be considered for the Scholars Program.[[138]](#cite_note-140)

### Feirstein Graduate School of Cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Feirstein Graduate School of Cinema")]

Main article: [Feirstein Graduate School of Cinema](/wiki/Feirstein_Graduate_School_of_Cinema "Feirstein Graduate School of Cinema")

Barry R. Feirstein Graduate School of Cinema is the first public graduate film school in New York City. It is the only film school in America to have its own classroom on a film lot with the collaboration of [Steiner Studios](/wiki/Steiner_Studios "Steiner Studios"), the largest soundstage on the East Coast. The program offers a two-year M.A. in Cinema Studies, a two-year M.F.A. in Cinema Arts in the discipline of [Producing](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer"), and a three-year M.F.A in Cinema Arts with five disciplines of [cinematography](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography"),
[directing](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production"), [screenwriting](/wiki/Screenwriting "Screenwriting"), and Digital Arts and Visual Effects. The school opened in the fall of 2015. The first graduating class was in Spring 2018.

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Brooklyn College athletic teams are nicknamed the Bulldogs. The college is a member at the [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") level of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA); primarily competing in the [City University of New York Athletic Conference](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York_Athletic_Conference "City University of New York Athletic Conference") (CUNYAC) since the 1996–97 academic year (which they also competed in a previous stint from 1978–79 to 1979–80). The Bulldogs previously competed in the [East Coast Conference](/wiki/East_Coast_Conference_(Division_I) "East Coast Conference (Division I)") at the [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level during the 1991–92 academic year.

Men's sports include basketball, cheerleading, cross country, soccer, swimming & diving, tennis and volleyball; while women's sports include basketball, cheerleading, cross country, soccer, softball, swimming & diving, tennis and volleyball. Their basketball program would find infamy for being the first reported incident of a potential bribe to fix a game against the Municipal University of Akron (now just the [University of Akron](/wiki/University_of_Akron "University of Akron")) on January 29, 1945, leading to five arrests against Brooklyn College's players in question.[[144]](#cite_note-146) Another former basketball player of theirs, a previous captain named David Budin, would later be involved as a part of the [1961 NCAA University Division men's basketball gambling scandal](/wiki/1961_NCAA_University_Division_men%27s_basketball_gambling_scandal "1961 NCAA University Division men's basketball gambling scandal") as a late addition for [Jack Molinas](/wiki/Jack_Molinas "Jack Molinas")' rigging group in question.[[145]](#cite_note-147) From 1971 to 1977, future Olympic coach [Michel Sebastiani](/wiki/Michel_Sebastiani "Michel Sebastiani") was the head fencing coach of the men's fencing team.[[146]](#cite_note-auto1aa-148)  The football field was used for the outdoor scenes in the 1978 adult film [Debbie Does Dallas](/wiki/Debbie_Does_Dallas "Debbie Does Dallas").[[147]](#cite_note-149)

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_College&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Mascot")]

In 2010, Brooklyn College adopted the Bulldog as its new mascot.[[148]](#cite_note-150) The athletic program was originally known as the Kingsmen. In 1994, the mascot was changed to the Bridges. However, after the school built new facilities and underwent other changes the athletic director pushed for a new name to reflect the new program.[[149]](#cite_note-151)
